coredata.dc:title: Efficient preparation strategy of high quantum yield multicolor CDs for warm white LED
coredata.dc:creator: Bai, Haiyan
Jin, Xilang
Ma, Xuehao
Zhao, Yaoxiao
Wang, Haozhe
Yu, Jiajia
Ding, Liu
Wei, Chenhui
Zhou, Hongwei
Chen, Weixing
coredata.dc:description: Multicolor carbon dots (CDs) possess tremendous potential applications, especially in optoelectronic devices. However, further applications in warm white LED have been constrained due to the extremely low quantum yield (QY) of multicolor CDs. In this work, fluorescence enhancement multicolor CDs (o-CDs(cat.) (emitted yellow, Em = 550 nm, QY = 85.4 %), m-CDs(cat.) (emitted blue, Em = 440 nm, QY = 35.7 %), p-CDs(cat.) (emitted red, Em = 610 nm, QY = 73.8 %)) with almost 15 % QY improvement, without changing the fluorescence emission wavelength, was successfully achieved by introducing MnSO4 into the reaction system. The relevant mechanism may be the catalytic effect of MnSO4, which leads to the increased content of C = N and pyridine N, further suppressing non radiative transitions, leading to the increasement of QY. Finally, the obtained high QY multicolor CDs were applied to warm white LED with the color coordinates (CIE) of (0.33,0.36), color rendering index (CRI) of 90, correlated color temperature (CCT) of 3473 K. Laying the foundation for the research methods and mechanisms of metal ion caused high QY of CDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: High quantum yield
multicolor CDs
Nonradiative transitions
MnSO4 catalyze
Warm white LED

Mechanism exploration of fluorescence enhanced CDs(cat.)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of high QY multicolor CDs(cat).
Fluorescence comparison of CDs and CDs(cat.) of (a) o-CDs; (b) m-CDs; (c) p-CDs, Fluorescence decay comparison of CDs and CDs(cat.) of (d) o-CDs; (e) m-CDs; (f) p-CDs.

UV–vis absorption, PL excitation spectrum, PL emission spectrum of (a) o-CDs(cat.); (b) m-CDs(cat.); (c) p-CDs(cat.), Fluorescence intensity Contour diagram of (d) o-CDs(cat.); (e) m-CDs(cat.); (f) p-CDs(cat.).

TEM of (a) o-CDs(cat.) (b) m-CDs(cat.) (c) p-CDs(cat.), size distribution diagrams of CDs were shown inside the graph with a scale of 20 nm; (d) XRD of the CDs(cat.); (e) Raman of the CDs(cat.); (f) The corresponding normalized maximum emission spectra of the CDs(cat.).

XPS survey comparison of CDs and CDs(cat.) of (a) o-CDs(cat.); (b) m-CDs(cat.); (c) p-CDs(cat.), CDs high-resolution of XPS (d) C 1s spectra; (e) N 1s spectra; (f) O 1s spectra, CDs(cat.) high-resolution of XPS (g) C 1s spectra; (h) N 1s spectra; (i) O 1s spectra,

(a) Optimized structures and frontier molecular orbitals (isosurface = 0.02 a.u.) of ground-state molecules on the calculation level of M06-2X/def2-SV of the CDs and CDs(cat.). (b) adiabatic excitation energy Ead(eV) from S0 to S1 and non-radiative transition rate constant
k
NR
(s−1). of the CDs and CDs(cat).
CIE coordinates of (a) B-LED, (b) Y-LED, (c) R-LED, (g) W-LED, Emission spectra of operating (d) B-LED, (e) Y-LED, (f) R-LED, (g) CIE of W-LED; (h) Working image of W-LED; (i) EL diagram e of W-LED.
The specific values of CDs and CDs(cat).

head.ce:title: Efficient preparation strategy of high quantum yield multicolor CDs for warm white LED

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials and Chemical Engineering, Xi’an Technological University, Xi’an 710021, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials and Chemical Engineering, Xi’an Technological University, Xi’an 710021, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Engineering Research Center of Light Stabilizers for Polymer Materials, Universities of Shaanxi Province, Xi’an, Shaanxi Province, 710021, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Engineering Research Center of Light Stabilizers for Polymer Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Engineering Research Center of Light Stabilizers for Polymer Materials, Universities of Shaanxi Province, Xi’an, Shaanxi Province, 710021, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Materials and Chemical Engineering, Xi'an Technological University, Xi'an 710032, Shaanxi, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Multicolor carbon dots (CDs) possess tremendous potential applications, especially in optoelectronic devices. However, further applications in warm white LED have been constrained due to the extremely low quantum yield (QY) of multicolor CDs. In this work, fluorescence enhancement multicolor CDs (o-CDs(cat.) (emitted yellow, Em = 550 nm, QY = 85.4 %), m-CDs(cat.) (emitted blue, Em = 440 nm, QY = 35.7 %), p-CDs(cat.) (emitted red, Em = 610 nm, QY = 73.8 %)) with almost 15 % QY improvement, without changing the fluorescence emission wavelength, was successfully achieved by introducing MnSO4 into the reaction system. The relevant mechanism may be the catalytic effect of MnSO4, which leads to the increased content of C = N and pyridine N, further suppressing non radiative transitions, leading to the increasement of QY. Finally, the obtained high QY multicolor CDs were applied to warm white LED with the color coordinates (CIE) of (0.33,0.36), color rendering index (CRI) of 90, correlated color temperature (CCT) of 3473 K. Laying the foundation for the research methods and mechanisms of metal ion caused high QY of CDs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: White light-emitting diodes (WLEDs) are the most prospective high-tech fields of the 21st century owing to their compact volume, simple structure, long operational life, and are almost suitable for all indoor and outdoor lighting [1–5]. There are three main strategies to realize the white light emission of phosphor-converted LEDs, blue LED chips coupled with yellow emissive phosphor, ultraviolet (UV) chips coupled with red, green, and blue (RGB) emissive phosphors, and UV chips coupled with white emissive phosphor. Especially the WLEDs made of RGB phosphors combined with UV chips exhibit adjustable performance of CCT and CRIs, making it an ideal candidate for WLEDs [6–10].

Multicolor photoluminescent (PL) materials [11–15], as emerging carbon nanomaterials, have been regarded as promising alternatives for LEDs [16–18]. In recent years, researchers have proposed various synthesis routes or reaction methods to meet the synthesis of multicolor CDs, including solvent regulation [19,20], element doping [21–25], pH regulation[26–30], and so on[31–34]. However, the low quantum yield (QY) of multicolor CDs limits their application in LEDs. Many researchers made efforts to improve the QY of the CDs. Wu and his coworkers [35] reported N, S co-doped carbon dots (N, S-CDs), the corresponding QY could be greatly enhanced from 11.2 % to 65.1 % and the fluorescence emission of CDs was red-shifted (430 nm to 545 nm). Liu and his coworkers reported yellow emissive CDs, the addition of manganese ions increased the degree of conjugated sp2-domians and surface states, further enhancing QY up to 68.6 % from 46 %, and it was applied to yellow LEDs [36]. However, the reported measurement only targeted monochrome CDs, and the wavelength shifted while enhancing QY. Therefore, it is necessary to study the universal methods caused by metal ions for QY enhancement of multicolor CDs without altering the fluorescence wavelength.

To achieve CDs with high QY, MnSO4 was introduced into the precursors, isomeric phenylenediamine was used as the precursor to synthesize multicolor CDs. CDs with higher QY were successfully prepared (o-CDs(cat.) (QY = 85.4 %), m-CDs(cat.) (QY = 35.7 %), p-CDs(cat.) (QY = 73.8 %)) in this work. Furthermore, we also found that MnSO4 acts as a catalyst in the system, which increased the content of C = N and pyridine N, further suppressing non radiative transitions, leading to the increasement of QY. Then, the high QY multicolor CDs were successfully applied to construct the warm white LEDs. The schematic diagram of multicolor CDs was shown in Fig. 1 .
Materials and measurements were shown in SI.

0.05 g phenylenediamine, 0.0151 g (0.1 mmol) MnSO4 were dissolved in 10 ml of ethanol, and sonicated for 20 min, then transferred into a 25 ml polytetrafluoroethylene reactor and reacted for 8 h at 200 °C. After that, the suspension was filtered by a 0.22 μm microporous membrane to get CDs(cat.) solution. The purified solution was dried to obtain solid-state CDs(cat.) for further testing (o-phenylenediamine and MnSO4 for o-CDs(cat.), m-phenylenediamine and MnSO4 for m-CDs(cat.), p-phenylenediamine and MnSO4 for p-CDs(cat.)). All the synthetic methods of CDs were similar, except that the 0.0151 g MnSO4 was not added to the system (o-phenylenediamine for o-CDs, m-phenylenediamine for m-CDs, p-phenylenediamine for p-CDs), The yield of CDs was shown in the Table S1 .

The CDs were prepared with the same method unless a gradient of MnSO4 content (0.005 g, 0.015 g, 0.02 g, 0.025 g) was set for the exploration of optimal MnSO4 content. Name them o-CDs-C1(m-CDs-C1, p-CDs-C1), o-CDs-C2(m-CDs-C2, p-CDs-C2), o-CDs-C3(m-CDs-C3, p-CDs-C3), o-CDs-C4(m-CDs-C4, p-CDs-C4) respectively.

In order to investigate the solvent stability of CDs(cat.), we took 0.1 ml of synthesized CDs(cat.) and diluted them with 5 ml of different solvents (MeOH, DMF, DCM, PE, EA, EtOH) for fluorescence performance testing.

To investigate the thermal stability of CDs(cat.), we took 0.1 ml of synthesized CDs(cat.) and diluted them with 5 ml of EtOH, Then, the obtained samples were placed in hydrothermal reaction vessels and kept at temperatures of 20 °C, 40 °C, 60 °C, and 80 °C for 5 h for fluorescence performance testing.

To investigate the metal ion quenching effect of CDs(cat.), we took 0.1 ml of prepared CDs(cat.) and 0.1 ml of 0.2 μM metal ion solutions (Na+, Mg2+, Fe2+, Zn2+, Cu2+, and blank control), 5 ml ethanol were added for fluorescence performance testing.

In order to further investigate the thermal stability of CDs(cat.)@strach, the prepared CDs(cat.)@strach solids were stored at constant temperatures of 50 °C, 100 °C, 150 °C, and 200 °C for 5 h for fluorescence performance testing.

The ground state geometry is optimized using DFT, and the excited states are calculated with linear response time-dependent DFT (TD-DFT) at the optimized ground state geometry. All calculations are performed with the Gaussian 16 package (Rev. C.01) using the CAM-B3LYP functional and the 6-311G* basis set. Grimme's D3BJ dispersion correction was used to improve the calculation accuracy.

Optimizations and frequency calculations of six molecules with the ground state (S0) and excited state (S1) were carried out on M06-2X/def2-SV without symmetry limitations under the polarizable continuum model (PCM) with ethanol solution (CH3CH2OH; ε = 24.852) via BDF program [37,38]. Nonadiabatic coupling matrix element (NACME) was further considered for all of the molecules. There was no imaginary frequency for all optimized molecules. Frontier molecular orbitals were shown with the isosurface value of 0.015 a. u.. MOMAP package [39] was used to get the fluorescence non-radiation rate on the temperature of 300 K.

After evenly mixing 3 g of starch with 2 ml of CDs(cat.) solution, adding 2 ml of ethanol for dilution, drying at 40 °C to obtain CDs(cat.)@starch. After mixing the CDs(cat.)@starch with AB silicone, coating it on a 400 nm ultraviolet lamp chip and drying at 80 °C, the LED lamp was successfully constructed (m-CDs(cat.)@starch for B-LED, o-CDs(cat.)@starch for Y-LED, p-CDs(cat.)@starch for R-LED, W-CDs(cat.)@starch for W-LED).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optics properties of fluorescence enhanced CDs(cat.)
ce:section.ce:para: In this work, multicolor CDs were synthesized by solvothermal method, phenylenediamine isomers as precursors and ethanol as solvent. On this basis, MnSO4 was selected for the fluorescence enhancement of multicolor CDs. We further investigated the effect of MnSO4 content on the fluorescence performance of CDs(cat.) ( Fig. S1 ), and obtained the optimal amount of MnSO4 added in the reaction system was 0.015 g. The results showed that MnSO4 could successfully achieve fluorescence enhancement of multicolor CDs without shifting the fluorescence wavelength. The optics properties were shown in Fig. 2 .

The UV–Vis absorption spectra of the o-CDs(cat.), m-CDs(cat.), and p-CDs(cat.) showed similar absorption peaks at 242 nm, and 294 nm, shown in Fig. 3
(a–c) (the left vertical axis). The peak at 242 nm was related to the π-π* electron transition of C = C in sp2 conjugation, and the peak at 294 nm was caused by the n-π* electron transition of C = O or C = N. The optimal PL excitation peaks of o-CDs(cat.), m-CDs(cat.), and p-CDs(cat.) were located at 430 nm, 357 nm, and 500 nm. The optimal PL emission peaks of o-CDs(cat.), m-CDs(cat.), and p-CDs(cat.) were located at 550 nm, 440 nm, and 610 nm (Fig. 3a–c) (the right vertical axis). Excitation emission 3D matrices for o-CDs(cat.), m-CDs(cat.), and p-CDs(cat.) were also shown (Fig. 3d–f). When the excitation wavelength was changed, the emission wavelength of the CDs(cat.) had minor changes within a small range. And it could be seen from the graph that the optimal emission wavelength of o-CDs (cat.) was about 550 nm, the optimal emission wavelength of m-CDs (cat.) was about 440 nm, and the optimal emission wavelength of p-CDs (cat.) was about 610 nm. The UV–Vis absorption, the optimal PL excitation and emission peaks of the CDs ( Fig. S5 ) were almost the same as that of CDs(cat.). A comparison of CDs synthesized by various methods using different precursors were shown in Table S2 . Compared with other CDs, and high quantum yield CDs were achieved using MnSO4 as catalyzer.

To further demonstrate the structure of CDs(cat.), TEM, XRD and Raman spectra were carried out. All three kinds of CDs(cat.) presented the uniform distribution of morphology, the average size of the synthesized CDs(cat.) was 2.12 nm (p-CDs(cat.)), 2.05 nm (o-CDs(cat.)) and 1.83 nm (m-CDs(cat.)), respectively, shown in Fig. 4 a–c. There was an increase in particle size from m-CDs(cat.) to o-CDs(cat.) to p-CDs(cat.). The XRD test was carried out to verify whether the CDs(cat.) had obvious conjugated structures (Fig. 4d). The peak positions located at 22° for the three kinds of CDs belonged to the (002) crystal plane of graphite phase carbon. This indicated that the CDs(cat.) had obvious conjugated structures[40]. However, there was no significant peak of Mn in the XRD pattern, indicating that MnSO4 may only play a catalytic role and does not participate in the structure of CDs(cat.). The Raman spectra of the three CDs(cat.) were presented in Fig. 4e. All three kinds of CDs(cat.) showed obvious D-band (1353 cm−1) and G-band (1561 cm−1). The IG/ID of p-CDs(cat.), o-CDs(cat.), m-CDs(cat.) was 1.08, 1.03 and 1.00, respectively. Which confirmed that the order of the decreased size of the sp2 conjugated domain was m-CDs(cat.), o-CDs(cat.) and p-CDs(cat.)[41,42]. Considering that the CDs(cat.) emitted red (Em = 610 nm), yellow (Em = 550 nm) and blue (Em = 440 nm) in turns (Fig. 4f), it could be concluded that the fluorescence wavelength red shifts took place with the increased size of sp2 conjugated domain in CDs(cat.).

Furthermore, the TEM, XRD and Raman of CDs were tested for structure comparation with CDs(cat.). CDs presented a uniform distribution of morphology, the average size was calculated to be 2.02 nm (p-CDs), 1.81 nm (o-CDs) and 1.71 nm (m-CDs) (Fig. S6a–c), smaller than that of the corresponding CDs(cat.). The XRD of CDs were presented in Fig. S6d, proved that CDs and CDs(cat.) had the similar obvious graphite phase structures. The IG/ID of p-CDs, o-CDs, m-CDs was calculated to be 1.06, 0.96 and 0.94, respectively (Fig. S6e), smaller than that of the corresponding CDs(cat.). Indicating that the sp2 conjugate domain of CDs(cat.) was larger than those of CDs. Considering that the CDs emitted red (Em = 610 nm), yellow (Em = 550 nm) and blue (Em = 440 nm) in turns (Fig. S6f), it could be concluded that the fluorescence wavelength red shifts took place with the increased size of sp2 conjugated domain in CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism exploration of fluorescence enhanced CDs(cat.)
ce:section.ce:para:
As is well known, nonradiative transitions play a decisive role in the QY of CDs.
k R
(radiation rate constants) and
k
NR
(non-radiation rate constants) were calculated for CDs and CDs(cat.) according to the following equations [35]:
(1)
k R
=
QY
τ
(2)
k
NR
=
( 1 - Q Y )
τ

The specific values of
k R
and
k
NR
were shown in Table 1
. Strikingly, the
k
NR
of CDs were smaller than that of CDs(cat.), indicating that nonradiative transitions were suppressed after introducing MnSO4 into the reaction system, leading to the increasement of fluorescence QY.

To investigate the causes of nonradiative transitions from a molecular structure perspective, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) of the CDs and CDs(cat.) were carried out. The FTIR results were shown in Fig. S7 , and the XPS results were shown in Fig. 5 .

The full spectrum specific results of XPS were shown in Fig. 5a–c. The peaks of the three constituent elements C1s, N1s, O1s were observed at about ∼284.2 eV, ∼398.9 eV, ∼531.8 eV for the CDs and CDs(cat.), respectively. We analyzed the element content of the CDs and CDs(cat.) ( Table S3 ). Mn and S element were not detected in the full survey of the CDs(cat.), demonstrating that MnSO4 may act as a catalyst, not involved in chemical reactions, and the results of EDS also confirmed this viewpoint [36,43] ( Figs. S8–S10 ). Furthermore, we conducted XPS analysis on the filter residue, and the results showed that the Mn element was present in the filter residue, accounting for 5 % of the total amount of filter residue. The results were shown in Table S4 .

The high-resolution C 1s spectrum of CDs (Fig. 5d) and CDs(cat.) (Fig. 5g) could be fitted into three peaks at about 283.5 eV, 284.4 eV, and 285.3 eV, which corresponded to C = N, C-N or C-O, and C-N bonds, respectively. The high-resolution N 1s spectrum of CDs (Fig. 5e) and CDs(cat.) (Fig. 5h) could be fitted into three peaks at about 397.7 eV, 398.4 eV, and 399.4 eV, which corresponded to pyridine N, graphite N, pyrrolic N, respectively. The high-resolution O 1s spectrum of CDs (Fig. 5f) and CDs(cat.) (Fig. 5i) could be fitted into two peaks at about 532.1 eV, and 533.9 eV, which correspond to the C = O bond and C-O bond, respectively. The content of each functional group could also be clearly shown in Fig. 5, when compared with CDs, the C = N and pyridine content of CDs(cat.) increased, the C = N content of o-CDs(cat.) increased from 26 % to 39.7 %, m-CDs(cat.) increased from 22.4 % to 32 %, p-CDs(cat.) increased from 32 % to 46.3 %, the pyridine N content of o-CDs(cat.) increased from 26.5 % to 30.9 %, m-CDs(cat.) increased from 20.6 % to 24.8 %, p-CDs(cat.) increased from 29.3 % to 37.6 %, which may suppress non radiative transitions. At the same time, the C = O content was also increasing. In order to further clarify the experimental process, we conducted XPS sampling tests at different time points during the reaction process, and the results were shown in Table S5 . Further indicating that the C = O content did not show a stable upward trend during the reaction process, but the C = N content show a stable upward trend. So, the influence of C = O on the increase of quantum yield could be excluded from the experimental perspective.

Based on the above, we could infer that MnSO4 may play a catalyzer role in the synthesis of CDs(cat.), which increased the content of C = N and pyridine N in the system, further suppressing nonradiative transitions, leading to the increase of QY [44–47].

From the result of experimental analysis, it could be observed that the conjugated domain of CDs gradually increases from m-CDs to o-CDs to p-CDs. From m-CDs (cat.) to o-CDs(cat.) to p-CDs(cat.), the conjugated domain of CDs gradually increases, and from CDs to CDs(cat.), the difference is the increase content of C = N and pyridine N. We construct the differences between the conjugated domains of CDs by increasing the number of aromatic rings [52,53], and increase the content of C = N and pyridine N in the system by introducing C = N into the conjugated structure (as shown in the upper row of Fig. 6 a). Firstly, the HOMO and LUMO energy levels were calculated based on the structures, and the results showed that the energy level decreased from m-CDs (5.42 eV) to o-CDs (4.34 eV) to p-CDs (2.28 eV), the same trend was also observed in m-CDs(cat.) (5.27 eV) to o-CDs(cat.) (4.23 eV) to p-CDs(cat.) (2.42 eV) (Fig. 6a), which proved that the main cause of polychromatic CDs was the increase of conjugated domains. Furthermore, the nonradiative transitions of the designed CDs were calculated (Fig. 6b). The results indicated that the nonradiative transition rate constant (
k
NR
) of CDs was higher than that of CDs(cat.), indicating that the nonradiative transitions would be suppressed by the increase of pyridine N content, leading to enhanced fluorescence of CDs. In order to theoretically excluded the influence of C = O bond on non-radiative transition inhibition, we designed a computational model and calculated the change in non-radiative transition rate constant under the introduction of C = O bond ( Fig. S13 ). The results indicated that as the C = O content increases, the non-radiative transition rate constant was not showed a decreasing trend. Therefore, the influence of changes in C = O content on non-radiative transitions could be excluded through theoretical calculations.

For fabricating LED, the mixtures of the CDs(cat.)@starch and AB-silicone were deposited on commercially available purple-emitting InGaN LED chips with an emission peak at 400 nm and solidified in an oven at 80 °C for 1 h. The LED chip was operated with a steady forward current of If = 100 mA and a low turn-on voltage of VL = 3.17 V. The corresponding CIE color coordinates of the CDs(cat.)-based LEDs (o-LED in (0.44, 0.47), m-LED in (0.21, 0.17), p-LED in (0.48, 0. 49)) were described in Fig. 7 a–c.

Then, the three CDs(cat.) were mixed in a weight ratio of 1:18:1 (o-CDs(cat.):m-CDs(cat.):p-CDs(cat.)) (the optimal performance obtained by adjusting the proportion was detailed in Fig. S15 ) for W-CDs(cat.), using the same fabrication method, W-LED devices with warm white light emission were created by depositing the mixture of W-CDs(cat.)@starch and AB-siloxane on InGaN LED chips. Some performance characterizations and fluorescence thermal stability of w-CDs(cat.)@strach were shown in Figs. S16–S17
.The FL spectrum of the warm W-LED, which ranged from 420 to 700 nm, was a white light signal almost covering the entire visible spectrum. The CRI of the warm W-LED was 90, the CCT and CIE of 3473 K and (0.33, 0.36), respectively (Fig. 7g), indicated that the warm W-LED produced high-quality illumination with a low CCT (CCT < 4000 K). The results indicated that the synthesized CDs(cat.) could be used in multicolor LED and would have excellent development in the field of photoluminescence.

To explore polychromatic CDs, isomeric phenylenediamine was used as the precursor to successfully synthesize multicolor CDs, p-phenylenediamine for o-CDs (emitted yellow), m-phenylenediamine for m-CDs (emitted blue), and p-phenylenediamine for p-CDs (emitted red), respectively. With the introduction of MnSO4 into the precursor, the QY and light intensity of CDs (o-CDs(cat.) (QY = 85.4 %), m-CDs(cat.) (QY = 35.7 %), p-CDs(cat.) (QY = 73.8 %)) were successfully improved, and the fluorescence intensity of CDs(cat.) was enhanced by the suppression of nonradiative transitions with the increasing content of C = N and pyridine N in the system. Using fluorescence enhanced polychromatic CDs, the warm white LED was successfully constructed. The strategy lays a foundation for the research methods and mechanisms of metal ion’s contribution to the high QY of CDs.

This work was financially supported by Technology Innovation Leading Program of Shaanxi (No. 2020QFY07-05) and the Innovative Talents Promotion Plan-Young Science and Technology Star Project (No. 2022KJXX-88).

Haiyan Bai: Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation. Xilang Jin: Supervision, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – review & editing. Xuehao Ma: Data curation, Methodology. Yaoxiao Zhao: Software, Formal analysis. Haozhe Wang: Methodology, Data curation. Jiajia Yu: Project administration, Methodology. Liu Ding: Validation, Funding acquisition. Chenhui Wei: Formal analysis, Software. Hongwei Zhou: Methodology, Supervision. Weixing Chen: Data curation, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2024.150980.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
